title: Directed extended-range percolation
authors: Wenbo Liu, Yiwen Zeng, Xueming Liu, Ginestra Bianconi
abstract: While for standard percolation directionality is known to increase the combinatorial complexity of percolation, here we show that when connectivity is ensured by paths of length $R\geq 2$, network directionality, impeding backtracking, can significantly reduce the complexity of percolation. To illustrate this finding, we introduce Directed Extended-Range Percolation (DERP), defined directed networks with non-reciprocal edges, motivated by applications in quantum communication. In this framework, message transmission is enabled between trusted nodes separated by a directed path of length at most $R$. Using a message-passing approach, we show that directionality enables an exact determination of the percolation threshold and the anomalous critical indices on locally tree-like structures. On random directed networks we find that the critical behavior of DERP depends sensitively on degree correlations. These analytical predictions are corroborated by extensive Monte Carlo simulations, highlighting the profound impact of directionality and correlations on long-range connectivity in complex networks.
summary: This paper explores a new model of directed extended-range percolation inspired by quantum communication. It demonstrates that network directionality can simplify the percolation problem and that degree correlations can lead to a vanishing percolation threshold in scale-free networks.
main_problem: The paper investigates how network directionality and degree correlations affect the percolation threshold and critical behavior in extended-range percolation models.
main_result: The authors show that directionality reduces combinatorial complexity and that in maximally correlated scale-free networks, the percolation threshold can vanish for certain power-law exponents.
method: The study uses a message-passing approach and linearization of recursive equations to derive analytical solutions, which are then validated using Monte Carlo simulations.
model_or_system: The model is Directed Extended-Range Percolation (DERP), where connectivity between trusted nodes is enabled by directed paths of maximum length R, potentially using untrusted nodes as relays.
key_observables: Percolation threshold (pc), order parameters (S, U, S(+), U(+)), and critical exponents (beta, beta_S, beta_U).
important_parameters: Interaction range (R), power-law exponent (gamma), and degree correlations (Uncorrelated vs. Maximally Correlated).
main_assumptions: The analytical derivation assumes locally tree-like structures and omits specific discussion of logarithmic corrections at the critical point gamma = 3.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the DERP mechanism; Figure 3 compares simulation results with theoretical predictions for order parameters; Figure 4 shows the dependence of the threshold on parameters; and supplementary figures show effective exponent plateaus and scaling plots.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the DERP model and its motivation, develops a message-passing framework for analytical solutions, analyzes critical behavior across different network topologies (UC and MC), and validates results via Monte Carlo simulations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. DERP message passing mechanism and net- work components.(a) Schematic of the DERP connectivity mechanism. Node i is trusted (filled circle), while the unfilled circles are untrusted relay nodes. If both m < R and n < R, node i connects to the SCGC through incoming and outgoing paths within range R and thus belongs to the SCGC together with the relay nodes. If only m < R (only n < R), node i and the corresponding relay nodes belong to the IGC (OGC). Spa- tial distribution of IGC, OGC, and SCGC in a representative network for R = 2 (b) and R = 3 (c), showing that increasing the interaction range expands all three giant components.

FIG. 2. DERP dynamics in a real-world p2p net- work [26] (N = 10, 876, R = 2). Symbols denote numerical simulations, and solid lines represent theoretical predictions. Despite the inherent network asymmetry causing the sizes of IGC and OGC to be unequal, the theoretical fits remain ex- cellent.

FIG. 3. DERP dynamics in directed networks with varying degree correlations. Comparison of simulations (symbols) and theoretical predictions (solid lines) for UC (Poisson) (a) and MC (Poisson) (b) networks. The order pa- rameters S and U are shown as functions of p for R = 2. Network parameters are N = 20,000, c = 2.5. Data are aver- aged over 100 realizations.

FIG. 4. Dependence of the critical exponent β on the parameter γ for R = 2 in the DERP model. Critical exponent β as a function of γ for R = 2 in the DERP model. (a) In the UC (PL) model, a single transition occurs at γ = 3 (vertical dashed line). For γ > 3, the system exhibits mean-field behavior. (b) In the MC (PL) model, four exponents (βS, βU, β± S , and β± U ) show boundaries at γ = 3 and γ = 4, becoming non-analytic at γ = 3. In both panels, symbols represent numerical simulations (N = 50, 000), and solid lines denote theoretical curves.
